_source: TH_EXPLORE_IT_DATA.json (authoritative structured data). Real accessions, per-item licence + open_access flag. Nothing authored by an assistant.

station.tagline: He financed the first stock exchange the world ever built, and he'd do it again tonight.

museum_objects.title: Gold Guilder of Willem V
museum_objects.description: An early Dutch gold guilder coin from the reign of Count Willem V of Holland.
museum_objects.significance: Before there were banks or exchanges, there was just this: a count deciding his gold coin would be the standard everyone in his territory trusted.
museum_objects.plain_language: An early Dutch gold coin from centuries before Amsterdam became a financial superpower.

resolved.via: TIER 2 — free API key required (CEO signup)

museum_objects.rights_note: NO OPEN IMAGE FOUND — panel must show text/citation only.

museum_objects.title: VOC Share Certificate
museum_objects.description: The oldest known share certificate in the world, issued by the Dutch East India Company.
museum_objects.significance: This piece of paper is the actual birth certificate of the modern stock market — the moment ownership of a company first became something you could buy, sell, and trade like a coin.
museum_objects.plain_language: The oldest stock certificate in the entire world — the very first time a company let ordinary people buy a piece of it.

resolved.warning: TERTIARY SOURCE — acceptable as a pointer, NOT as TH's evidence. Replace with the primary source it cites.

museum_objects.title: Regents of the Old Men's Almshouse
museum_objects.description: A Frans Hals group portrait of the civic regents who administered Dutch charitable and financial institutions.
museum_objects.significance: These are the faces of the men who ran Amsterdam's money — stern, wealthy, and utterly certain the whole world was watching how they managed it.
museum_objects.plain_language: A famous painting of the wealthy men who managed Amsterdam's charities and finances during the Dutch Golden Age.

display_image.title: An old man crying into a tunic while his sons pull it away from him

museum_objects.title: VOC Coinage
museum_objects.description: Coins minted specifically for use by the Dutch East India Company across its Asian trading network.
museum_objects.significance: Money made not for a country, but for a company — proof that by Lars's era, corporations had become powerful enough to need their own currency.
museum_objects.plain_language: Special coins made by a private company, not a government, to use in its trading posts across Asia.
museum_objects.rights_note: NO OPEN IMAGE FOUND — panel must show text/citation only.

museum_objects.title: Bank of Amsterdam Ledger Records
museum_objects.description: Surviving administrative ledgers from the Bank of Amsterdam, one of the world's first central banks.
museum_objects.significance: Every entry in these ledgers is a tiny proof that trust, once written down carefully enough, can move more money than gold ever could.
museum_objects.plain_language: Old handwritten account books from one of the world's first central banks.

primary_sources.title: Oldest Share Certificate
primary_sources.author: Guinness World Records
primary_sources.description: Official record confirming the VOC 1606 certificate as the world's oldest surviving share.
primary_sources.plain_language: The official world record confirming this really is the oldest stock certificate ever found.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

primary_sources.title: The Bank of Amsterdam and the Governance of Money
primary_sources.author: De Nederlandsche Bank (DNB) Working Paper 696
primary_sources.description: Central bank research paper analyzing the monetary policy and governance of the historic Bank of Amsterdam.
primary_sources.plain_language: A modern central bank's own research paper studying how its 400-year-old ancestor actually worked.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

primary_sources.title: VOC Company Records Archive
primary_sources.author: Dutch East India Company / National Archives
primary_sources.description: Corporate and financial records of the VOC, the world's first multinational corporation.
primary_sources.plain_language: The actual surviving business records of the world's first major global corporation.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

primary_sources.title: History of the Amsterdam Stock Exchange
primary_sources.author: Various economic historians
primary_sources.description: Scholarly accounts of the founding of the Amsterdam Beurs, the first formal stock exchange.
primary_sources.plain_language: Historical research on how Amsterdam built the world's first true stock exchange building.

primary_sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
primary_sources.description: Overview reference on the VOC's founding, structure, and financial innovations.
primary_sources.plain_language: A general overview of the company that basically invented the modern stock market.

world_map.map_description: Lars's guilder built the world's first stock exchange in Amsterdam, then followed Dutch trading ships to colonies and ports across four continents.

nodes.label: Amsterdam Beurs
nodes.description: Site of the world's first formal stock exchange, where VOC shares were traded starting in 1611.

nodes.label: Dordrecht
nodes.description: An early center of Dutch trade and minting before Amsterdam's rise to dominance.

nodes.label: New Amsterdam (Manhattan)
nodes.description: The Dutch colonial settlement that became New York City, carrying guilder-based trade across the Atlantic.

nodes.label: Cape Town
nodes.description: A key VOC resupply station on the route to Asia, extending Dutch financial reach to Africa.

nodes.label: Dejima, Japan
nodes.description: The only point of contact between Japan and the West for over two centuries, run by the VOC.

nodes.label: Batavia (Jakarta)
nodes.description: The VOC's Asian headquarters, the commercial hub of the entire Dutch trading empire.
